results.title: 10 Painful ways enterprise AI Assistants frustrate employees
results.content:
Approval chains, SLAs, change windows, and catalog rules often live in ITSM workflows, governance tools, and static policy docs.

AI assistants stumble because these rules aren't always structured, accessible, or consistent across systems, making it hard for them to reason if it is this allowed before acting.

## 5. The Momentum killer: "Why can't I raise this issue on Slack?"

The culprit: No or low channel coverage. ‍

"Why do I need to switch to the portal? Can't you just handle it on Slack?"

AI assistants often frustrate users by limiting where and how they can interact. If an employee starts a request in Slack, Teams, or email, they expect the request to be handled right there, without breaking the momentum of their work. [...] But most AI assistants live inside a portal, or only work in chat, or force users to switch to email or portal after answering a few initial queries. Each system logs activity differently, making it hard for AI to recognize that the same user is making the same request in a different place. Without this, the user experience feels fragmented: one channel gives you knowledge, another gives you forms, another just tells you to "go somewhere else."

At Atomicwork, we launched universal agent earlier this year just to solve for this. Employees should be able to raise and resolve issues on channels of their choice, whether it's their browser, Slack, Teams, or email.

results.title: Salesforce’s Marc Benioff Uses AI to Stay On Top Of Employee Complaints
results.content:
Speaking broadly about AI’s impact on employee surveillance, Amba Kak, executive director of the AI Now Institute at New York University, expressed her concerns to the outlet.

“It results in a chilling effect on what people are saying in the workplace,” Kak said. She noted that the Federal Trade Commission, Justice Department and Equal Employment Opportunity Commission have all shared apprehensions about the technology at large. “These are as much worker rights issues as they are privacy issues.”

## Key Takeaways

If you’re venting to co-workers about your workplace frustrations, you should probably take it offline. Or at least off the company Slack channel. [...] You're viewing the US website. Looking for the Site edition?

# Salesforce CEO Marc Benioff Uses AI to Stay On Top Of Employee Complaints

Benioff reveals how he learns about employee challenges.

Copied to clipboard

## Key Takeaways

If you’re venting to co-workers about your workplace frustrations, you should probably take it offline. Or at least off the company Slack channel.

Salesforce CEO Marc Benioff just revealed how he uses the AI tool, Slackbot, to scan companywide Slack channels in an episode of the All-In podcast, per Business Insider.

The CEO, whose company acquired Slack for $27.7 billion in 2021, harnesses Slackbot to uncover company issues and operational opportunities.

results.title: How an AI Coworker in Slack Changed the Way Our Company Works
results.content:
It holds the messiest context. Code, docs, and metrics are structured — an agent can pull them from anywhere. Discussion isn’t: questions, replies, and half-made decisions are scattered across channels and threads. An agent in Slack pulls them together on the spot, with nothing to organize first.
   It’s where everyone already is. Every other tool — GitHub, Asana, HubSpot — has its own slice of users. The chat platform is the exception: almost everyone keeps it open all day, which makes it the lowest-friction home for an agent. @-ing Matt is no different from tagging a colleague. [...] Sitemap

Open in app

Sign up

Sign in



Development: our agentic development loop. In Slack, Matt works through a feature’s design and requirements with us, then implements it directly. We’ve even let non-engineers deliver features this way. It also runs regular system health checks, and because customer issues and alerts feed into Slack, it can open an investigation the moment one lands instead of waiting for an engineer to free up. We’ve built agent automation into each stage of the cycle (design, build, monitor, fix), wherever it can carry the work, with engineers reviewing and adjusting from there.

results.title: Your boss’s AI may already be reading your Slack messages
results.content:
BYSarah Bregel

Audio 1

Listen to this ArticleMore info

0:00 / 0:00

Video 1

Video 2

AI wears many hats at work, whether it’s a brainstorming partner, a mentor, or the one doing the actual work. However, it’s time to add a new one to the list: spy. In a recent episode of the _All-In_ podcast, Salesforce CEO Marc Benioff said he was using AI to analyze employee Slack messages to understand what they’re complaining about. (Salesforce acquired Slack in 2021.)

“Because you run your company on Slack, all your DMs, all your channels, we’re reading that now through the AI, and we can tell you more about your business than you know,” Benioff explained. “Slackbot is reading stuff that, you know, nobody knew what was happening. I’m using that myself.” [...] 2-for-1 Innovation Festival Passes Are Now LiveImage 1: close banner
